questions.q: Who chaired the boundary commission that drew the line dividing India and Pakistan in 1947?
questions.explanation: Sir Cyril Radcliffe chaired the Boundary Commissions, and the border drawn under his direction became known as the Radcliffe Line.

questions.q: The Radcliffe Line forms the boundary between which two countries?
questions.explanation: The Radcliffe Line demarcated the border between newly independent India and Pakistan in 1947.

questions.q: Which two major provinces were partitioned by the Radcliffe Line?
questions.explanation: The Radcliffe Line cut through the two provinces of Punjab in the west and Bengal in the east.

questions.q: In which year was the Radcliffe Line drawn and published?
questions.explanation: The Radcliffe Line was demarcated and its award published in August 1947, around the time of independence.

questions.q: What was Sir Cyril Radcliffe's profession before he was appointed to draw the boundary?
questions.explanation: Cyril Radcliffe was a British barrister, chosen partly because he had never been to India and was seen as impartial.

questions.q: The hasty drawing of the Radcliffe Line contributed to which mass event after independence?
questions.explanation: The line's hurried demarcation displaced millions and was followed by huge migrations and communal violence during Partition.

questions.q: Before his appointment, had Sir Cyril Radcliffe ever visited India?
options: Yes, he had lived there for years
Yes, he was born in India
Yes, he had served as a judge there
No, he had never been to India
questions.explanation: Radcliffe had never visited India before, a fact intended to make him neutral but which also limited his local knowledge.

questions.q: The Radcliffe Line in the east separated which Pakistani province from India?
questions.explanation: In the east the line separated East Bengal, which became East Pakistan and later Bangladesh, from India.

questions.q: What broad criterion was the Boundary Commission meant to use in drawing the line?
options: Areas of Muslim and non-Muslim majority
questions.explanation: The commission was instructed to divide territory chiefly on the basis of contiguous Muslim and non-Muslim majority areas.

questions.q: The phrase often used to describe the Radcliffe Line emphasizes that it was drawn in a span of about:
questions.explanation: Radcliffe had only about five weeks to complete the boundary award, hence the image of a border 'drawn in weeks.'

questions.q: Roughly when was the Radcliffe Boundary Award officially made public?
questions.explanation: The award was announced on 17 August 1947, two days after the formal independence of India and Pakistan.

questions.q: Under which 1947 act were the boundary commissions established to partition India?
questions.explanation: The Indian Independence Act 1947 provided the legal framework for partition and the boundary commissions.

questions.q: Apart from the Punjab commission, Radcliffe also chaired the boundary commission for which province?
questions.explanation: Radcliffe chaired both the Punjab and the Bengal Boundary Commissions that divided those provinces.

questions.q: The contested allotment of the Gurdaspur district largely to India had implications for access to which region?
questions.explanation: The award of much of Gurdaspur to India gave India a land link toward Jammu and Kashmir, a point long debated by historians.

questions.q: Each boundary commission, besides Radcliffe as chairman, included how many other members?
options: Four, two nominated by each of Congress and the Muslim League
questions.explanation: Each commission had four other members, two nominated by the Congress and two by the Muslim League, with Radcliffe as the deciding chairman.

questions.q: Why were the boundary commission members other than Radcliffe often unable to reach agreement?
options: They split evenly along Congress and League lines
questions.explanation: The Indian members typically divided along Congress and Muslim League positions, leaving Radcliffe to make the final decisions.

questions.q: Which city, awarded to Pakistan, had been the venue of the 1929 Congress session and the 1940 Muslim League resolution?
questions.explanation: Lahore, a major contested city, went to Pakistan under the Radcliffe award, while nearby Amritsar remained in India.

questions.q: A key difficulty in dividing Bengal was the location of which industrial resource near Calcutta?
options: Jute mills and the port of Calcutta
questions.explanation: Calcutta's jute mills lay in India while much of the jute-growing land went to East Pakistan, splitting an integrated economy.

questions.q: It is widely reported that after completing his task, Radcliffe did what with his fee or papers?
options: Published a detailed memoir at once
Sold his maps to a museum
Returned to India to oversee the line
Declined his fee and later destroyed his working papers
questions.explanation: Radcliffe reportedly declined his salary for the work and destroyed his papers, distressed by the consequences of the partition.

questions.q: The poet W. H. Auden later wrote a poem reflecting critically on Radcliffe's task, titled:
questions.explanation: Auden's poem 'Partition' depicts Radcliffe drawing the boundary in haste with inadequate information.

questions.q: The Radcliffe Line left the Chittagong Hill Tracts, with a largely non-Muslim population, on which side?
questions.explanation: Despite a largely non-Muslim tribal population, the Chittagong Hill Tracts were awarded to East Pakistan, a much-criticized decision.

questions.q: Which senior British official, as Viceroy, oversaw the broader partition process within which the Radcliffe award was made?
questions.explanation: Lord Mountbatten was the last Viceroy and presided over the partition arrangements that included the Radcliffe Line.

questions.q: In which year was Cyril Radcliffe born, the barrister who became the namesake of the partition border?
questions.explanation: Cyril Radcliffe was born in 1899 and was in his late forties when he undertook the boundary work in 1947.

questions.q: The Bengal district of Murshidabad, with a Muslim majority, was awarded to India largely because:
options: Control of the Hooghly headwaters mattered to Calcutta
It was demanded by the Muslim League
questions.explanation: Muslim-majority Murshidabad went to India, partly to keep control of the Ganges-Hooghly river system feeding Calcutta, while Hindu-majority Khulna went to Pakistan.

questions.q: It is often noted that the boundary awards were finalized before independence but their publication was delayed until afterward, a decision attributed to:
options: the Muslim League boycotting the announcement
Mountbatten, to avoid disorder marring the independence celebrations
questions.explanation: The award's release was held back until after 15 August, a move generally attributed to Mountbatten's wish to keep the transfer ceremonies untroubled.

questions.q: Which Sikh-revered town in Punjab was a focal point of dispute, given the community's distribution across the line?
questions.explanation: Nankana Sahib, the birthplace of Guru Nanak, fell on the Pakistani side, while the Golden Temple city of Amritsar remained in India, deeply affecting Sikhs.

questions.q: The Radcliffe Line did NOT settle the boundary of which princely state, leaving a long-running dispute?
questions.explanation: Princely states like Jammu and Kashmir were outside the commission's remit, and Kashmir's status became a lasting dispute between India and Pakistan.

questions.q: Approximately how much time did Radcliffe have from his arrival in India to deliver the boundary award?
questions.explanation: Radcliffe arrived in early July 1947 and had roughly five to six weeks to draw boundaries affecting tens of millions of people.

questions.q: The decades-long enclave problem along the eastern border, partly rooted in the 1947 settlement, was largely resolved by which 2015 agreement?
questions.explanation: The tangled enclaves left along the line were largely settled by the India-Bangladesh Land Boundary Agreement implemented in 2015.

questions.q: Which Punjab district, with its Muslim majority and complex canal systems, became one of the most contested awards in the west?
questions.explanation: Gurdaspur's partial award to India was among the most contested western decisions because of its mixed population and strategic location.
